status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: Language learning app with spaced repetition, speech recognition for pronunciation, conversational AI practice, gamification, and offline mode.

plan.description: Define business requirements, user personas (children under 13, adult learners, educators), user stories, and ROI model. Document COPPA/FERPA applicability based on target age groups and institutional use.

payload.coppa_scope: Determine if under-13 users are in scope and document parental consent flows

acceptance_criteria: At least 30 user stories written and prioritized by MoSCoW
COPPA applicability decision documented with legal rationale
FERPA scope documented for any institutional/educator use cases
ROI model includes 3 monetization scenarios (freemium, subscription, institutional)

plan.description: Analyze competitors (Duolingo, Babbel, Rosetta Stone, Pimsleur), identify differentiators for spaced repetition + speech AI combo, define target languages and markets.
acceptance_criteria: Feature matrix comparing top 5 competitors across all key dimensions
Differentiation statement for speech + conversational AI positioning
Go-to-market strategy for at least 2 language markets
Pricing benchmark analysis against 3 competitors

plan.description: Write the Product Requirements Document (PRD), define MVP scope, create phased roadmap (MVP → v1.0 → v2.0), and establish success metrics (DAU, lesson completion rate, pronunciation accuracy threshold, retention at 30/60/90 days).
acceptance_criteria: PRD covers all 5 core features with functional requirements and edge cases
MVP scope agreed with at least 15 user stories tagged as MVP
3-phase roadmap with milestone dates and success criteria per phase
OKRs defined for first 6 months post-launch

plan.description: Conduct user research, create information architecture, design wireframes for all core flows (onboarding, daily lesson, pronunciation drill, conversational AI session, progress dashboard, offline mode), and produce accessibility audit plan for WCAG 2.1 AA compliance.

payload.child_safe_design: Simplified UI variant for under-13 users, no social features visible

acceptance_criteria: Wireframes completed for all 7 core flows in low and high fidelity
WCAG 2.1 AA checklist documented with pass/fail per component
Child-safe UI variant wireframes for COPPA-applicable screens
Usability test plan with at least 5 test participants per persona
Accessibility audit report with remediation backlog
Accessibility for learners with disabilities
Mobile-responsive for student devices

plan.description: Produce compliance evidence artifacts for COPPA, FERPA, and WCAG 2.1. Document parental consent workflows, data minimization policies for minors, FERPA data handling for institutional users, and create traceability matrix mapping features to compliance requirements.
acceptance_criteria: COPPA compliance checklist completed with evidence for each requirement
Parental consent flow documented with verification mechanism (email, credit card micro-charge, or knowledge-based)
FERPA data handling agreement template drafted for institutional customers
WCAG 2.1 AA traceability matrix maps every UI component to compliance status
Privacy Impact Assessment completed and reviewed by legal_advisor

plan.description: Draft Terms of Service, Privacy Policy (COPPA-compliant with parental consent section), Cookie Policy, and institutional licensing agreement (FERPA-aware). Review IP ownership of user-generated speech data.

payload.speech_data_note: Clarify ownership and retention of user pronunciation recordings

acceptance_criteria: Privacy Policy includes explicit COPPA section with parental rights and consent mechanism
ToS includes FERPA data processing addendum for institutional users
Speech data IP policy clearly states user owns recordings, company has limited processing license
GDPR data subject rights documented (access, deletion, portability)
Cookie consent mechanism specified for GDPR compliance
FERPA/COPPA compliance if minors involved

plan.description: Set up project monorepo structure (mobile app + backend + ML services), configure development toolchain (React Native or Flutter for mobile, FastAPI backend, Python ML services), CI/CD skeleton, environment configs, and agentic AI pattern configuration for conversational practice (coordinator pattern with specialist sub-agents for grammar, vocabulary, and pronunciation coaching).

coordinator_components.task_router: intent classifier routes user message to appropriate specialist
coordinator_components.result_merger: synthesizes multi-agent responses into coherent conversational reply

acceptance_criteria: Monorepo initialized with all service directories and shared type definitions
Coordinator agentic pattern scaffolded with 4 specialist agent stubs
Feature flag system operational for gradual rollout
All environment configs validated with no secrets committed
Pre-commit hooks installed (lint, type check, secret scan)

plan.description: Design and implement database schema for users, vocabulary items, learning sessions, spaced repetition state (SM-2 or FSRS algorithm state per card per user), gamification (streaks, XP, badges, leaderboard), speech recordings metadata, and conversation history.

tables: user_card_state (SRS state per user per card)
payload.srs_algorithm: FSRS (Free Spaced Repetition Scheduler) — stores stability, difficulty, due_date, review_count
payload.offline_sync: Include sync_state and last_synced_at columns on all user-owned tables
payload.child_data_isolation: Separate schema or tenant for under-13 users with enhanced access controls

acceptance_criteria: ERD documented covering all entities and relationships
FSRS card state schema supports stability, difficulty, due_date, last_review, review_count
All user-owned tables include sync_state column for offline support
Under-13 user data isolation mechanism implemented and tested
Migrations run cleanly from empty DB to full schema
Indexes on high-frequency query paths (user_id+due_date for SRS reviews)

plan.description: Design OpenAPI specification for all backend endpoints: auth (with parental consent flow), user management, SRS review queue, lesson content delivery, speech evaluation submission, conversational AI session management, gamification events, and offline sync endpoints.

payload.auth: JWT with refresh tokens, parental consent token for under-13
payload.offline_sync_protocol: Delta sync with vector clocks for conflict resolution

acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI 3.1 spec covers all endpoint groups with request/response schemas
Auth flow includes parental consent endpoint with verification mechanism
Offline sync endpoints support delta-only payloads with conflict resolution strategy documented
All endpoints document error codes including COPPA-specific errors
API contract reviewed and approved before backend implementation begins

plan.description: Implement core backend: FastAPI application with JWT auth, parental consent flow, user management service, COPPA age-gate middleware, FERPA institutional data isolation, and audit logging for all PII access.

services: AuditService (PII access log for FERPA compliance)
payload.parental_consent: Email verification flow with 7-day pending state before account activation for under-13
payload.security: bcrypt password hashing, PKCE for OAuth, PII fields encrypted at rest

acceptance_criteria: Age gate rejects under-13 registration without verified parental consent
Parental consent email flow functional end-to-end in staging
All PII access logged with user_id, accessor_id, timestamp, and purpose
FERPA institutional users isolated in separate tenant context
All auth endpoints return correct HTTP status codes and OWASP-safe error messages
JWT expiry and refresh token rotation working correctly

plan.description: Implement spaced repetition engine service using the FSRS algorithm. Expose endpoints for fetching due review cards, submitting review ratings (0-3 scale), and computing next review schedule. Support bulk offline sync of review sessions.

payload.offline_sync: Accept batched offline review sessions with timestamps, replay in order
payload.deck_management: Pre-built language decks + user custom decks

acceptance_criteria: FSRS algorithm produces correct next-due dates for all 4 rating values (verified against reference implementation)
Due card queue returns cards sorted by due_date with configurable daily limit
Offline batch sync correctly replays sessions in chronological order without data loss
Algorithm state persists across sessions and survives service restart
Performance: due-card query for user with 5000 cards completes in < 100ms

plan.description: Build speech recognition and pronunciation evaluation pipeline. Integrate Whisper (offline-capable) for transcription and a phoneme-level scoring model to give pronunciation feedback. Deploy as a microservice with async job queue for evaluation results.

payload.transcription_model: OpenAI Whisper (medium model for accuracy, base model for on-device offline)
payload.pronunciation_scoring: Phoneme-level comparison using Montreal Forced Aligner or similar — scores per phoneme, overall word score, and specific feedback (e.g. 'your R sounds like L')
payload.on_device_model: Whisper base (39M params) bundled in mobile app for offline mode
payload.evaluation_pipeline: Audio upload → transcription → phoneme alignment → score computation → structured feedback JSON
payload.feedback_format: { word: str, score: 0-100, phoneme_breakdown: [{phoneme, expected, actual, score}], tip: str }

acceptance_criteria: Whisper transcription achieves < 5% WER on clean speech for all 6 target languages
Pronunciation scoring returns phoneme-level breakdown with actionable tip per word
On-device Whisper base model bundled and functional offline on iOS and Android
Evaluation pipeline processes 10-second audio clip in < 3 seconds server-side
Model evaluation report documents WER and pronunciation scoring accuracy benchmarks

plan.description: Implement the conversational AI practice system using the Coordinator agentic pattern. Build ConversationalCoordinator that routes user messages to specialist sub-agents (GrammarCoach, VocabularyCoach, PronunciationCoach, CulturalContextAgent), synthesizes responses, and maintains conversation history with learning context injection.

coordinator_agent.responsibility: Classify user intent, route to specialist agents, synthesize final response, maintain conversation state

specialist_agents.GrammarCoach: Detects and explains grammatical errors in user messages with target-language examples
specialist_agents.VocabularyCoach: Suggests vocabulary improvements, introduces new words from SRS deck, tracks usage
specialist_agents.PronunciationCoach: Flags words for next pronunciation drill session, references prior speech scores
specialist_agents.CulturalContextAgent: Adds cultural nuance and appropriate register (formal/informal) guidance

payload.history_management: Sliding window of 20 turns + summarized long-term context in vector store

acceptance_criteria: Coordinator correctly routes to all 4 specialist agents based on message intent
Specialist agent responses synthesized into single coherent reply in < 4 seconds
Conversation history persists across sessions and injects relevant prior context
4 conversation modes all functional and produce distinct agent behavior
Grammar errors flagged inline without breaking conversation flow
Vocabulary introduced from user's SRS deck (not random) at appropriate intervals

plan.description: Implement gamification service: XP system, streak tracking, badge/achievement engine, and weekly leaderboard. Support offline-earned XP sync. Implement anti-cheat validation for streak freeze purchases and XP events.

payload.streaks: Daily streak with streak freeze (purchasable or earnable), timezone-aware midnight reset
payload.leaderboard: Weekly XP leaderboard, friend groups, and global — resets Sunday midnight UTC
payload.anti_cheat: Server-side XP validation, max XP cap per session type, rate limiting on XP events

acceptance_criteria: XP correctly awarded for all 6 event types with server-side validation
Streak resets at midnight user-local timezone, not UTC
Streak freeze correctly applied when user misses a day
Badge engine awards all 5 badge types at correct thresholds
Weekly leaderboard resets correctly and archives prior week
Offline-earned XP synced on reconnect without duplication

plan.description: Implement offline sync service: content download manager (lesson packs, SRS decks, on-device Whisper model), delta sync protocol for review sessions and XP events, and conflict resolution using vector clocks. Support partial pack downloads and resumable downloads.

payload.sync_protocol: Delta sync: client sends last_sync_timestamp, server returns changes since. Vector clocks for conflict resolution.
payload.conflict_resolution: Last-write-wins for XP events; merge (take max) for streak state; SRS state uses server-wins with local queue replay
payload.storage_estimate: Full language pack: ~50MB audio + 5MB text. Whisper base: ~150MB per language.
payload.resumable_downloads: Chunked downloads with byte-range support, download state persisted locally

acceptance_criteria: Full lesson pack for one language downloads and is functional offline
Delta sync sends only changed records since last_sync_timestamp
Vector clock conflict resolution tested with 3 conflict scenarios (offline edits on 2 devices)
Resumable download resumes from interruption point without re-downloading completed chunks
Sync completes for 500 pending reviews in < 10 seconds on WiFi

plan.description: Create user-facing onboarding guides, in-app tutorial overlays, educator setup documentation for FERPA institutional accounts, and video scripts for app store preview videos.

acceptance_criteria: 10 help center articles cover all major features with screenshots

plan.description: Write technical documentation: API reference (auto-generated from OpenAPI spec), architecture decision records (ADRs) for FSRS algorithm choice, coordinator agentic pattern, offline sync protocol, and SBOM documentation.

payload.api_docs: Auto-generated from OpenAPI spec + human-written usage examples per endpoint group
adrs: ADR-001: FSRS over SM-2 for spaced repetition
ADR-002: Coordinator pattern for conversational AI
ADR-003: Whisper base on-device for offline speech
ADR-004: Vector clock conflict resolution for offline sync
payload.architecture_overview: System diagram, data flow diagrams, component boundaries
payload.runbook: On-call runbook for speech evaluation service degradation, sync conflicts spike, COPPA data deletion requests

acceptance_criteria: API reference covers all endpoints with at least one request/response example each
4 ADRs written with context, decision, consequences, and alternatives considered
Architecture diagram accurately reflects deployed system components
On-call runbook covers top 3 incident scenarios with step-by-step resolution
COPPA data deletion runbook documents process and SLA (must complete within 45 days per FTC)

flaws: CRITICAL: Email verification does NOT constitute 'verifiable parental consent' under COPPA. The FTC explicitly requires methods such as a signed consent form, credit card micro-charge, knowledge-based authentication, or government ID. An email flow will fail FTC review and expose the company to civil penalties up to $51,744 per violation. This invalidates Step 10's core auth implementation.

CRITICAL: Zero content creation pipeline. The plan builds a sophisticated engine with no mechanism to produce the actual learning content — vocabulary decks, professionally-recorded native speaker audio, lesson sequences, cultural context data. This is typically 40-60% of the cost and timeline for a language learning product. No CMS, no content ingestion pipeline, no editorial workflow.

Montreal Forced Aligner is a batch research tool, not a real-time scoring engine. MFA requires dictionary lookups and G2P models per language, processes audio in sequential steps (not pipelined), and has limited phoneme dictionaries for Arabic and Japanese. The '< 3 seconds server-side' target is not achievable with MFA for all 6 languages, particularly for Arabic.

Whisper base is a single multilingual model (~140MB), not a per-language model. Stating '~150MB per language' is a fundamental misunderstanding of the architecture. The storage estimate for offline is incorrect, and the download manager UI (Step 21) will show wrong sizes. More critically, Whisper base's WER on Arabic is ~15-25% — the '< 5% WER for all 6 target languages' acceptance criterion is unachievable with the base model.

On-device Whisper in a React Native/Expo managed workflow requires native modules (llama.cpp bindings or similar). This creates a hard conflict with Expo managed workflow — bare workflow is required, which significantly increases CI/CD complexity (EAS Build custom native modules, platform-specific builds). Step 7 picks React Native + Expo without resolving this tension.

FSRS without per-user parameter optimization is significantly less effective than advertised. The real value of FSRS-4.5 over SM-2 is per-user weight optimization from review history. The plan uses global default parameters, which degrades to near SM-2 quality for new users and never personalizes. No mention of the FSRS optimizer running on user review history.

Coordinator pattern with 4 sequential specialist agent calls will routinely exceed the 4-second response target. Each LLM call is 1-3 seconds at production load. GrammarCoach + VocabularyCoach + synthesis alone hits the ceiling, before adding PronunciationCoach context lookup and CulturalContextAgent retrieval.

Offline pronunciation scoring is deferred to sync (Step 18) but Step 26 requires 'pronunciation drill score and retry' as a passing E2E journey. These are contradictory: the user gets no phoneme feedback offline, breaking the core offline value proposition for pronunciation.

Vector clock conflict resolution for FSRS state (Step 15) is architecturally unsound. FSRS state is path-dependent — the stability value after rating 'Hard' on a card with stability=5 differs from replaying the same rating on server-updated state=7. 'Server-wins with local queue replay' will produce different scheduling than what the user experienced, causing phantom reviews and broken stability estimates.

No subscription/payment backend. The ROI model (Step 1) includes freemium and subscription scenarios, but no step implements in-app purchases (StoreKit for iOS, Google Play Billing), subscription state management, or paywall enforcement. In-app purchase integration with Apple/Google is a significant workload with compliance requirements of its own.

No push notification infrastructure. Daily reminder notifications are the single highest-impact retention mechanism for language learning apps (Duolingo's entire retention strategy depends on this). Absent from every step.

App Store child-directed designation is unaddressed. Apps targeting under-13 users must register under Apple's 'Made for Kids' category and Google Play's 'Designed for Families' program. These programs restrict third-party SDKs (no Facebook, many analytics), enforce COPPA-compliant data handling at the platform level, and require separate review. The CI/CD plan (Step 27) ignores this entirely.

No LLM cost model for conversational AI at scale. 4 specialist agents × average 500 tokens × cost per token × DAU × sessions/day = potentially $50K-$200K/month at modest scale. No discussion of cost controls, token budget per session, caching, or which LLM provider is used.
suggestions: Replace email-only parental consent with a compliant COPPA verification service (Veriff, PRIVO, or SuperAwesome) — these are purpose-built SDKs that handle the FTC's acceptable verification methods and stay current with regulatory changes. Budget 2-4 weeks for integration.

Add a Step 2.5 or Step 3.5 for content architecture: define the content data model, identify native speaker recording vendors (Voices.com, Speechify Studio, in-house studio), build a content ingestion pipeline, and plan the initial deck sizes per language. Without this, Steps 11, 17, and 18 have no data to work with.

Replace Montreal Forced Aligner with a purpose-built pronunciation assessment API for production (SpeechAce, Azure Pronunciation Assessment, or Google Speech-to-Text with word confidence scores). Use MFA only for offline/research evaluation. Revisit phoneme-level scoring architecture around these APIs' actual output formats.

Switch to Expo bare workflow immediately in Step 7 or accept the limitation that on-device Whisper will not be available in the MVP. Document this trade-off explicitly in an ADR. If bare workflow, plan for separate iOS/Android CI lanes with native dependency caching.

Add FSRS optimizer as a background job in Step 11: collect minimum 50 reviews per user, run parameter optimization weekly, store personalized weights per user. This is the core differentiator over SM-2 and should be in v1.0 not v2.0.

Cap the conversational AI response at 2 specialist agent calls per turn maximum (route to the most relevant specialist only, not all 4). Add a response streaming SSE endpoint so perceived latency is lower even if total generation time is 5-6 seconds.

Add Step 10.5: Payment/subscription service — StoreKit 2 (iOS) + Google Play Billing Library 5+ (Android), server-side receipt validation, subscription state webhook handlers, entitlement management. This blocks the freemium model entirely if absent.

Add Step 7.5: Push notification infrastructure — FCM + APNs integration, notification permission flow (critical for under-13: must be parent-consented), streak reminder scheduling, and unsubscribe management for GDPR compliance.

In the COPPA compliance step (Step 5), add explicit Apple MfK / Google DFF registration requirements and the SDK allow-list review — many analytics SDKs (Firebase Analytics, Mixpanel) are prohibited in child-directed contexts without special data processing agreements.

Add a content management system step between Steps 3 and 7: admin panel for deck management, content approval workflow, audio upload + normalization pipeline, and localization of lesson content (separate from app UI i18n in Step 22).
missing: Content creation pipeline: vocabulary deck production, native speaker audio recording, lesson sequencing, and cultural content for 6 languages. This is the product's core asset and has no step.
In-app purchase and subscription management (StoreKit 2, Google Play Billing, server-side receipt validation, entitlement service).
Push notification infrastructure (FCM, APNs, permission flow, streak reminders, GDPR opt-out).
CMS / admin panel for content managers and educators to manage decks, lessons, and student accounts.
Analytics product instrumentation (event schema, Amplitude/Mixpanel integration, funnel analysis setup) — CloudWatch alone is insufficient for product decisions.
LLM provider selection and cost model for conversational AI: which model, what token limits per session, cost per conversation turn, monthly budget ceiling.
App Store submission requirements for child-directed apps: Apple Made for Kids category, Google Designed for Families program, SDK restrictions imposed by each.
GDPR data portability export format — what file format, which fields, delivery mechanism (email, in-app download), and SLA.
Whisper model language-specific performance benchmarks BEFORE committing to the < 5% WER target. Arabic and Mandarin with the base model will fail this criterion.
Customer support tooling and COPPA data deletion request intake — the runbook in Step 29 mentions a 45-day SLA but there's no support ticket system or intake form planned.
FSRS per-user parameter optimization job — a core differentiator entirely absent from the algorithm implementation.
Pronunciation reference audio recording plan — who records native speaker audio for thousands of vocabulary items across 6 languages, at what quality (studio vs. crowdsourced), at what cost.
security_risks: Children's speech recordings stored in S3 without explicit field-level access controls. S3 bucket policy + presigned URL expiry is insufficient if object keys are guessable (e.g., user_id/recording_id). COPPA violation risk: unauthorized access to minor's voice recordings. Require envelope encryption per recording with per-user KMS keys.

JWT storage in React Native: AsyncStorage is unencrypted and accessible to any JS code in the app. Refresh tokens in AsyncStorage are exfiltrable by XSS equivalents in WebView components or compromised native modules. Use iOS Keychain / Android Keystore via react-native-keychain for all token storage.

Offline XP manipulation window: XP events accumulated offline are replayed on sync with only 'server-side validation' as protection. An attacker can manipulate the local SQLite database (especially without SQLCipher, which is only mentioned for review, not guaranteed in implementation) to inject arbitrary XP events. Server must independently recompute achievable XP from session metadata, not trust client-reported values.

Parental consent endpoint (POST /auth/parental-consent) has no brute-force protection mentioned beyond general rate limiting. An attacker enumerating pending consent tokens (UUID-based or timestamp-predictable) could activate under-13 accounts without genuine parental action. Add HMAC-signed tokens with expiry and one-time use enforcement.

IDOR on speech recordings: POST /speech/evaluate and related endpoints must enforce that the requesting user_id matches the recording owner. The threat model lists this but the API spec (Step 9) has no explicit ownership enforcement requirement on the speech endpoints — it must be in the auth middleware, not assumed.

SBOM scan in CI without a remediation SLA. Generating a CycloneDX SBOM is listed as an acceptance criterion but there is no policy for what happens when a critical CVE is found — does the build block? Who is notified? For a children's app with COPPA exposure, a critical CVE in a data-handling library must block deployment.

No mention of SQL injection protection at the ORM layer. 'Parameterized queries' is listed in Step 23 but not enforced in Step 10's implementation acceptance criteria. FastAPI + SQLAlchemy with raw text() queries is a common injection vector — must be explicitly prohibited in coding standards.
final_review.summary: This is an ambitious and structurally well-organized plan that demonstrates genuine product depth — the FSRS algorithm choice, coordinator agentic pattern, vector clock sync, and compliance awareness all reflect serious thinking. However, it has two critical failures that require rework before any implementation begins: (1) the COPPA parental consent mechanism is legally invalid — email verification will not satisfy FTC requirements, exposing the company to significant penalties; and (2) there is no content creation pipeline, meaning the entire technical stack will have nothing to teach when it launches. Beyond these blockers, several technical assumptions are incorrect (MFA for real-time pronunciation, Whisper base WER targets for Arabic/Mandarin, per-language Whisper model sizing, Expo managed workflow + on-device ML compatibility), and major product components are entirely absent (payments, push notifications, CMS, app store child-directed registration). The plan scores 54: the architecture is sound enough to build from, but the compliance flaw is a launch-blocking legal risk, the missing content pipeline is a fundamental product gap, and roughly 6-8 steps need to be added before this plan reflects what it would actually take to ship.

flaws: CRITICAL: Email verification does NOT constitute 'verifiable parental consent' under COPPA. The FTC explicitly requires methods such as a signed consent form, credit card micro-charge, knowledge-based authentication, or government ID. An email flow will fail FTC review and expose the company to civil penalties up to $51,744 per violation. This invalidates Step 10's core auth implementation.

CRITICAL: Zero content creation pipeline. The plan builds a sophisticated engine with no mechanism to produce the actual learning content — vocabulary decks, professionally-recorded native speaker audio, lesson sequences, cultural context data. This is typically 40-60% of the cost and timeline for a language learning product. No CMS, no content ingestion pipeline, no editorial workflow.

Montreal Forced Aligner is a batch research tool, not a real-time scoring engine. MFA requires dictionary lookups and G2P models per language, processes audio in sequential steps (not pipelined), and has limited phoneme dictionaries for Arabic and Japanese. The '< 3 seconds server-side' target is not achievable with MFA for all 6 languages, particularly for Arabic.

Whisper base is a single multilingual model (~140MB), not a per-language model. Stating '~150MB per language' is a fundamental misunderstanding of the architecture. The storage estimate for offline is incorrect, and the download manager UI (Step 21) will show wrong sizes. More critically, Whisper base's WER on Arabic is ~15-25% — the '< 5% WER for all 6 target languages' acceptance criterion is unachievable with the base model.

On-device Whisper in a React Native/Expo managed workflow requires native modules (llama.cpp bindings or similar). This creates a hard conflict with Expo managed workflow — bare workflow is required, which significantly increases CI/CD complexity (EAS Build custom native modules, platform-specific builds). Step 7 picks React Native + Expo without resolving this tension.

FSRS without per-user parameter optimization is significantly less effective than advertised. The real value of FSRS-4.5 over SM-2 is per-user weight optimization from review history. The plan uses global default parameters, which degrades to near SM-2 quality for new users and never personalizes. No mention of the FSRS optimizer running on user review history.

Coordinator pattern with 4 sequential specialist agent calls will routinely exceed the 4-second response target. Each LLM call is 1-3 seconds at production load. GrammarCoach + VocabularyCoach + synthesis alone hits the ceiling, before adding PronunciationCoach context lookup and CulturalContextAgent retrieval.

Offline pronunciation scoring is deferred to sync (Step 18) but Step 26 requires 'pronunciation drill score and retry' as a passing E2E journey. These are contradictory: the user gets no phoneme feedback offline, breaking the core offline value proposition for pronunciation.

Vector clock conflict resolution for FSRS state (Step 15) is architecturally unsound. FSRS state is path-dependent — the stability value after rating 'Hard' on a card with stability=5 differs from replaying the same rating on server-updated state=7. 'Server-wins with local queue replay' will produce different scheduling than what the user experienced, causing phantom reviews and broken stability estimates.

No subscription/payment backend. The ROI model (Step 1) includes freemium and subscription scenarios, but no step implements in-app purchases (StoreKit for iOS, Google Play Billing), subscription state management, or paywall enforcement. In-app purchase integration with Apple/Google is a significant workload with compliance requirements of its own.

No push notification infrastructure. Daily reminder notifications are the single highest-impact retention mechanism for language learning apps (Duolingo's entire retention strategy depends on this). Absent from every step.

App Store child-directed designation is unaddressed. Apps targeting under-13 users must register under Apple's 'Made for Kids' category and Google Play's 'Designed for Families' program. These programs restrict third-party SDKs (no Facebook, many analytics), enforce COPPA-compliant data handling at the platform level, and require separate review. The CI/CD plan (Step 27) ignores this entirely.

No LLM cost model for conversational AI at scale. 4 specialist agents × average 500 tokens × cost per token × DAU × sessions/day = potentially $50K-$200K/month at modest scale. No discussion of cost controls, token budget per session, caching, or which LLM provider is used.
suggestions: Replace email-only parental consent with a compliant COPPA verification service (Veriff, PRIVO, or SuperAwesome) — these are purpose-built SDKs that handle the FTC's acceptable verification methods and stay current with regulatory changes. Budget 2-4 weeks for integration.

Add a Step 2.5 or Step 3.5 for content architecture: define the content data model, identify native speaker recording vendors (Voices.com, Speechify Studio, in-house studio), build a content ingestion pipeline, and plan the initial deck sizes per language. Without this, Steps 11, 17, and 18 have no data to work with.

Replace Montreal Forced Aligner with a purpose-built pronunciation assessment API for production (SpeechAce, Azure Pronunciation Assessment, or Google Speech-to-Text with word confidence scores). Use MFA only for offline/research evaluation. Revisit phoneme-level scoring architecture around these APIs' actual output formats.

Switch to Expo bare workflow immediately in Step 7 or accept the limitation that on-device Whisper will not be available in the MVP. Document this trade-off explicitly in an ADR. If bare workflow, plan for separate iOS/Android CI lanes with native dependency caching.

Add FSRS optimizer as a background job in Step 11: collect minimum 50 reviews per user, run parameter optimization weekly, store personalized weights per user. This is the core differentiator over SM-2 and should be in v1.0 not v2.0.

Cap the conversational AI response at 2 specialist agent calls per turn maximum (route to the most relevant specialist only, not all 4). Add a response streaming SSE endpoint so perceived latency is lower even if total generation time is 5-6 seconds.

Add Step 10.5: Payment/subscription service — StoreKit 2 (iOS) + Google Play Billing Library 5+ (Android), server-side receipt validation, subscription state webhook handlers, entitlement management. This blocks the freemium model entirely if absent.

Add Step 7.5: Push notification infrastructure — FCM + APNs integration, notification permission flow (critical for under-13: must be parent-consented), streak reminder scheduling, and unsubscribe management for GDPR compliance.

In the COPPA compliance step (Step 5), add explicit Apple MfK / Google DFF registration requirements and the SDK allow-list review — many analytics SDKs (Firebase Analytics, Mixpanel) are prohibited in child-directed contexts without special data processing agreements.

Add a content management system step between Steps 3 and 7: admin panel for deck management, content approval workflow, audio upload + normalization pipeline, and localization of lesson content (separate from app UI i18n in Step 22).

history.summary: This is an ambitious and structurally well-organized plan that demonstrates genuine product depth — the FSRS algorithm choice, coordinator agentic pattern, vector clock sync, and compliance awareness all reflect serious thinking. However, it has two critical failures that require rework before any implementation begins: (1) the COPPA parental consent mechanism is legally invalid — email verification will not satisfy FTC requirements, exposing the company to significant penalties; and (2) there is no content creation pipeline, meaning the entire technical stack will have nothing to teach when it launches. Beyond these blockers, several technical assumptions are incorrect (MFA for real-time pronunciation, Whisper base WER targets for Arabic/Mandarin, per-language Whisper model sizing, Expo managed workflow + on-device ML compatibility), and major product components are entirely absent (payments, push notifications, CMS, app store child-directed registration). The plan scores 54: the architecture is sound enough to build from, but the compliance flaw is a launch-blocking legal risk, the missing content pipeline is a fundamental product gap, and roughly 6-8 steps need to be added before this plan reflects what it would actually take to ship.
